reason: STEP (ii) IS LOGICALLY SUPERFLUOUS FOR PROPERNESS: Step (ii) argues that |dot-gamma^i| < |dot-gamma^0| for each spatial i (which is correct: since each (dot-gamma^i)^2 is one non-negative summand in sum_{i=1}^3 (dot-gamma^i)^2 < (dot-gamma^0)^2, each term is strictly less than (dot-gamma^0)^2). It then concludes 'the spatial components remain bounded on the bounded tau-interval from (i).' But this growth-rate comparison is unnecessary: ANY continuous function is bounded on a bounded closed interval. Step (i) establishes gamma^{-1}(K) is contained in a bounded tau-interval, and step (iii) then needs gamma^{-1}(K) to be closed and bounded. The boundedness of gamma^{-1}(K) as a subset of R follows directly from step (i) (it is contained in a bounded interval), regardless of what gamma^i does. Step (ii) proves something about the IMAGE of gamma (bounded spatial coordinates), which is not needed for Heine-Borel applied to the DOMAIN. The properness argument needs: (a) gamma^{-1}(K) is bounded [step (i)], and (b) gamma^{-1}(K) is closed [step (iii)]. Step (ii) is a correct but vacuous intermediate step. Consider removing or repurposing it.
